interest_in_committee.why_are_you_intere.answer:
I am interested in serving on the Pennsylvania PreK Counts/Head Start Supplemental Assistance Program Advisory Committee because my entire professional life and much of my personal life have centered on improving access to high‑quality early childhood education for children and families who are often overlooked. I have spent the last 14 years working in early childhood programs in Pennsylvania—first as a Pre‑K Counts lead teacher in a school district classroom in York County, and currently as the Early Childhood Education Coordinator for a nonprofit that partners with both PKC and Head Start grantees across several counties (York, Adams, and Cumberland). I have seen first‑hand how these programs can completely change the trajectory of a child’s life when they are implemented well and adequately supported.

I also come to this work as a parent. My son, who is now in 4th grade, attended a PA PreK Counts classroom in the West Shore School District, and my niece is currently enrolled in a Head Start program in Dauphin County. Experiencing the system from both the provider side and the family side has given me a very concrete understanding of what is working and where families and providers are still struggling. For example, I have witnessed families lose their childcare arrangements because of changes in work schedules that did not align with rigid program hours, and I have supported teachers who were trying to implement high‑quality curricula while worrying about low wages and high turnover in their classrooms.

Serving on this Advisory Committee would give me the opportunity to bring those lived experiences, as well as data and lessons from my current coordination and coaching work, into a statewide conversation. I am particularly interested in helping to strengthen alignment between PreK Counts and Head Start so that children can transition smoothly between programs and into kindergarten, without families having to navigate two completely different sets of expectations and procedures. In my current role, I have helped two partner agencies braid PKC and Head Start funding in a single classroom model, and I have seen both the benefits and the challenges—especially in terms of staffing, documentation, and meeting both sets of performance standards.

I am also motivated by a strong commitment to equity. In the communities I work with, many of the families are working low‑wage jobs, are single parents, or are recent immigrants. I have supported families who were hesitant to enroll because of language barriers or past negative experiences with institutions. I want to make sure that the policies guiding PKC and HSSAP are responsive to these realities: that outreach materials are culturally and linguistically appropriate, that eligibility processes are as simple and dignified as possible, and that programs receive the support they need to truly include children with disabilities and children who have experienced trauma. I would like to contribute to discussions about how to better collect and use data on who is being served—and who is not—to inform funding and policy decisions.

Another reason I want to serve is that I believe the voices of classroom educators, family advocates, and local coordinators need to be present when statewide decisions are made. Too often, policies are crafted with good intentions but without a full understanding of their impact on day‑to‑day operations. For instance, I have helped programs navigate changes in staff qualification requirements and classroom ratios, and while I absolutely support high standards, I also see the strain these changes can place on already thin staffing pipelines. On the committee, I would aim to be a bridge between policymakers and practitioners, sharing concrete examples from classrooms and family conferences so that decisions are grounded in reality.

Finally, I am interested in supporting Pennsylvania’s efforts to build a more coherent early learning system overall. I have been involved in local quality improvement initiatives, such as Keystone STARS coaching and kindergarten transition collaboratives, and I see real potential for PKC and HSSAP to serve as anchors for broader community partnerships. I would welcome the opportunity to learn from colleagues across the state, share promising practices from south‑central Pennsylvania, and contribute to long‑range planning that keeps the needs of children and families at the center. Serving on this Advisory Committee feels like a natural next step in my service to the early childhood community and a way to amplify the experiences of the families and educators who have trusted me with their stories and their children’s futures.

relevant_experience_and_attributes.pract.answer:
In addition to my experience in center‑based, family child care, school district, and intermediate unit settings, I have worked extensively with community‑based early learning collaborations that do not fit neatly into one category. For three years, I coordinated a partnership between our local library system, a community health center, and a housing authority to provide pop‑up early learning sessions in low‑income apartment complexes and in the waiting rooms of pediatric clinics.

In these roles, I was responsible for adapting developmentally appropriate practices to very non‑traditional environments. For example, we created “laundromat literacy corners” in two neighborhood laundromats in York City, where we trained staff to engage children with simple story time activities while families were doing laundry. I also supported a faith‑based organization that ran a part‑day preschool ministry and wanted to align more closely with PA early learning standards and explore becoming a PKC partner in the future. This involved providing staff training, helping them conduct self‑assessments using the Environment Rating Scales, and coaching them on building stronger family engagement practices.

These experiences have given me insight into how early childhood services can be delivered in flexible, community‑driven ways outside of traditional classrooms, and how PKC and HSSAP might better connect with these informal or emerging early learning settings to create more seamless supports for families who may not yet be enrolled in formal programs.

relevant_experience_and_attributes.non_p.answer:
I currently serve on the board of directors for a small nonprofit in York County called Families First Early Learning Collaborative, which focuses on expanding access to high‑quality early childhood programs through scholarships and community partnerships. On this board, I chair the Program Quality Committee, where I review program data, help set quality benchmarks aligned with Keystone STARS, and participate in decisions about which providers to partner with based on their ability to serve children with higher needs.

Previously, I was a member of the Pennsylvania Association for the Education of Young Children (PennAEYC) Public Policy Committee from 2019 to 2023. In that role, I participated in monthly calls focused on statewide policy issues affecting PKC, Head Start, and child care. I helped review and provide feedback on PennAEYC’s policy briefs, including recommendations around workforce compensation and credential pathways. I also contributed to planning the early childhood advocacy day at the Capitol in Harrisburg, where we brought parents, teachers, and program directors to meet with legislators.

In addition, I served on a regional Kindergarten Transition Task Force convened by the Lincoln Intermediate Unit 12, which brought together representatives from school districts, Head Start, PreK Counts, and child care providers across three counties. Our group developed shared transition protocols and family information packets, and we piloted a process where preschool teachers shared child portfolios (with family consent) with receiving kindergarten teachers. These committee experiences have prepared me to work collaboratively in diverse groups, listen to multiple perspectives, and move toward consensus while keeping children’s needs at the center.

relevant_experience_and_attributes.other.answer:
Beyond the experiences listed above, I bring several additional attributes that I believe would be valuable to the Advisory Committee:

1. Bilingual and cross‑cultural experience: I am conversational in Spanish and have used this skill regularly to support Spanish‑speaking families in understanding enrollment processes, family handbooks, and individualized learning plans for their children. While I am not a certified interpreter, I have completed basic cultural competence and language access trainings, and I work closely with professional interpreters when discussing sensitive topics such as developmental concerns or eligibility. This has given me a strong appreciation for how language access policies and practices impact family engagement in PKC and Head Start classrooms.

2. Data literacy and continuous quality improvement: In my current role as Early Childhood Education Coordinator, I manage several data dashboards that track child assessment outcomes (using tools like Teaching Strategies GOLD), attendance patterns, family engagement participation, and staff professional development hours across multiple sites. I have led teams through Plan‑Do‑Study‑Act (PDSA) cycles to address specific challenges, such as chronic absenteeism or low CLASS scores in instructional support. I am comfortable interpreting data, asking critical questions about what it does and does not tell us, and using it to inform practical changes in classrooms and program policies.

3. Trauma‑informed and mental health collaboration: Over the past five years, I have partnered with an Early Childhood Mental Health Consultant to support classrooms that serve children who have experienced significant trauma, including family homelessness, foster care placement, and parental incarceration. I helped design staff trainings on trauma‑informed practices, supported teachers in creating calm‑down spaces, and participated in case conferences with family support staff and mental health providers. This work has shaped my belief that PKC and HSSAP policies must explicitly support mental health consultation and staff well‑being, not just focus on child outcomes.

4. Experience as both a teacher and administrator: Having started my career as a PreK Counts lead teacher and later moving into coaching and coordination roles, I understand the realities of implementing policies from both sides. I have had to write lesson plans that align with PA Early Learning Standards while also meeting Head Start Program Performance Standards, and I have also been the person responsible for ensuring that multiple classrooms are compliant with monitoring requirements and documentation expectations. This dual perspective helps me anticipate how proposed policy changes might affect classroom practice, paperwork burdens, and staff morale.

5. Commitment to authentic family voice: I have facilitated multiple parent advisory groups, including a PKC/Head Start blended classroom parent committee and a series of evening family leadership workshops. In these settings, I have learned how to create space for families to share honest feedback, including criticism of our programs, and how to bring their ideas forward in a way that leads to actual changes (for example, revising our communication methods to include more text messaging and videos, and adjusting family conference times to better accommodate shift workers). I would approach my role on the Advisory Committee with the same commitment—to lift up family perspectives, especially those of families who may not typically sit at decision‑making tables.
